impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award, finding sufficient evidence that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of patent-based tying can still defend by demonstrating that the tied product lacks substantial non-infringing uses, as this remains a viable defense that goes to the jury.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented goods face significant antitrust liability exposure, including damages, when the tied products have uses outside the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages based on foreign sales of domestically made software are recoverable under 35 U.S.C. § 271.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million patent infringement judgment against Norton (Gen Digital), holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions are abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that damages based on foreign sales were improperly awarded, requiring reconsideration of the damages model.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity method patent assertions benefit from this ruling, which narrows the scope of patent-eligible software claims and limits damages to domestic conduct.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with software method patents, particularly in cybersecurity, face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges, and universities and other licensors may see reduced damages when accused products are sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for tailoring media content delivery specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications for wireless devices are ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing the jury's $2.5 million infringement verdict against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device capabilities.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies benefit from this ruling reinforcing that patents claiming conventional content adaptation techniques for different devices are patent-ineligible, reducing exposure to infringement suits from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content delivery optimization and media streaming face increased invalidity risk under § 101, particularly when the claims recite server-side content tailoring at a high level of abstraction.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims arising from a patent applicant's alleged failure to disclose prior art documents acquired through a corporate acquisition.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether Tenaris's patent inventors intentionally withheld prior CYMAX documents from the USPTO. The case was remanded for trial on both the patent unenforceability defense and the attempted monopolization counterclaim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies that acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions are reminded that disclosure obligations to the USPTO are substantial, and competitors have viable paths to challenge patents obtained through alleged concealment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who obtained patents after corporate acquisitions face heightened litigation risk, as courts will allow inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust claims to proceed to trial when there is evidence of awareness of undisclosed prior art.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the trade remedies statute, and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The court directed the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industries benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, enabling better access to non-confidential information that may be relevant to their commercial interests.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations may see previously protected business information become publicly accessible, requiring more careful designation of truly confidential data.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding Board discretion to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under the APA. The court emphasized the Director's unreviewable statutory discretion over institution decisions and retained authority to override Board decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to flexibly manage IPR institution decisions without the constraints of formal rulemaking, potentially reducing duplicative patent challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and frequent IPR petitioners like Apple, Cisco, Google, and Intel face continued barriers to challenging the PTO's discretionary IPR denials, limiting their ability to use IPR as a cost-effective alternative to district court litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft, holding that the district court applied an overly rigid standard in excluding the expert's reasonable royalty analysis. The court emphasized that alleged flaws in a damages methodology go to the weight, not admissibility, of expert testimony.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more flexible standard for admitting damages expert testimony, reducing the risk of case-dispositive Daubert exclusions before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face greater difficulty in obtaining pre-trial dismissal through Daubert challenges to damages experts, potentially increasing litigation costs and settlement pressure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's technical and infringement experts and granting judgment as a matter of law of no induced infringement of surgical device patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two experts and grant of JMOL for DePuy in a patent case involving en bloc spinal derotation surgical techniques. The court found the exclusions were an abuse of discretion and remanded for a new trial, allowing both experts to testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs, particularly individual inventors, benefit from the Federal Circuit's pushback against aggressive Daubert gatekeeping that effectively disposes of infringement cases before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies defending induced infringement claims face increased trial risk, as appellate courts may reverse expert exclusions that appeared to resolve cases at summary judgment or JMOL.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claim terms in a crop harvester patent were indefinite when the corresponding structure was disclosed in the specification as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding of indefiniteness for the '395 patent's 'control means' limitation, holding that the specification's disclosure of conventional controller components (controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system) was sufficient corresponding structure even where described as 'conventional.' The case was remanded for further infringement proceedings against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more forgiving standard for means-plus-function disclosures, as conventional components described in the specification can constitute adequate corresponding structure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment and technology companies defending against means-plus-function patent claims face narrowed indefiniteness defenses when the specification identifies the structural components, even with minimal detail.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury's findings on non-obviousness and damages for a pre-lit artificial tree patent were supported by substantial evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL motion on obviousness and damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated electrical connections. The court upheld the jury verdict finding the patent non-obvious and sustained the damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from affirmance of jury verdicts on non-obviousness and damages where the accused infringer's challenges are largely factual.
impacts.negativeImplications: Manufacturers of consumer products who adopt patented features face sustained infringement liability and damages when jury verdicts on validity and infringement are supported by substantial evidence.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google, holding that claims of the '637 patent directed to web conferencing with time-shifting and asynchronous playback capabilities are patent-ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to abstract concepts without an inventive concept.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Google benefit from continued narrowing of patent-eligible subject matter for software claims involving conventional computing functions like recording, playback, and time-shifting.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and holders of software patents directed to web conferencing and media playback face increased risk of early dismissal under § 101.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's entry of a limited exclusion order under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) against defaulting respondents was proper, and whether the ITC's no-violation finding as to active respondents on trademark infringement and dilution claims was timely appealed.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely, but affirmed the ITC's entry of a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under Section 337(g)(1). The court held the ITC did not abuse its discretion in entering a limited rather than general exclusion order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can still obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting importers of infringing goods through the ITC's Section 337 process, even when substantive claims fail against active respondents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark holders seeking broad import exclusion through general exclusion orders face significant hurdles when the ITC finds no violation against active respondents, and strict appeal deadlines must be observed.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act when the SBA failed to award grants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by over 300 restaurants that the SBA violated the statutory mandate to award RRF grants in the order applications were received. The court held that the RRF statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in application order) creates an enforceable right to damages against the government.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses that applied for but did not receive government grant funds due to improper processing order may pursue damages claims in the Court of Federal Claims when the statute contains mandatory distribution language.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability to hundreds of businesses for failing to follow statutory grant distribution procedures, establishing precedent that mandatory statutory language in spending programs creates enforceable rights.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claim terms in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's obviousness determination was supported by substantial evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of DivX's streaming encryption patent and vacated the Board's finding that Netflix had not proven obviousness. The case was remanded for the Board to reconsider obviousness under the corrected construction, potentially invalidating DivX's patent.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies like Netflix benefit from corrected claim constructions in IPR proceedings that may lead to invalidation of asserted patents covering encrypted media delivery technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting streaming and DRM-related patents face increased risk that appellate claim construction reversals will undermine favorable Board decisions.
